indicators.description: Total net GHG emissions on Belgian territory (incl. LULUCF, excl. intl. aviation & sea transport). CO2, CH4, N2O and F-gases.
indicators.target_context: -55% vs 1990 baseline (142.8 MtCO₂eq)
indicators.policy: European Climate Law — Reg. (EU) 2021/1119; Paris Agreement (Art. 4)
indicators.data_source_description: Annual national GHG inventory totals 1990–2023, broken down by gas and sector
indicators.notes: WAM scenario projects 83.7 MtCO₂eq by 2030 — still well above 64.3 target. Court ruling Nov 2023 found targets legally insufficient.
indicators.consequences: ESR non-compliance: if Belgium misses annual emission allocations, it must purchase extra allowances from other MS or face tightened future targets (×1.08 penalty factor). Compliance checks every 5 years (2027, 2032). Continued shortfall can trigger EU infringement proceedings → referral to Court of Justice → financial fines. Belgium already faced infringement for late NECP submission (2024). Court ruling (Nov 2023) ordered accelerated action. No direct fine per tonne of CO2 missed, but political and financial pressure is significant.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Belgium as a whole is accountable to the EU (ESR). Internally, responsibility is split: Flanders, Wallonia and Brussels each have their own climate plans. A burden-sharing agreement divides the national ESR target. Brussels and Wallonia committed to -47%; Flanders only -40% (below national obligation). Federal government covers federal buildings, vehicles and makes up remaining gap. Coordinated via the Interministerial Conference on the Environment (ICE).
indicators.short_description: Belgium's total greenhouse gas emissions — currently 97.9 MtCO₂eq, far above the 2030 target of 64.3 Mt.

indicators.indicator: Renewable Energy Share (% of final consumption)
indicators.description: Share of energy from renewable sources in gross final energy consumption. Includes imported renewables and statistical transfers per RED accounting rules.
indicators.target_context: NECP target; EU indicative formula gives 33% for Belgium
indicators.policy: EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED III) — Directive (EU) 2023/2413
indicators.data_source_description: Annual renewable share of gross final energy consumption, EU harmonised methodology
indicators.notes: Belgium has one of the lowest RES shares in EU. Figure includes imported renewable electricity and statistical transfers — not purely domestic production. No separate domestic production target exists.
indicators.consequences: Under RED III, failure to meet the 2030 RES target can trigger EU infringement proceedings. Belgium already subject to infringement for incomplete RED transposition (INFR 2021/0145). If Belgium misses its national contribution, it must make 'statistical transfers' — paying other MS for their surplus renewable energy. This is costly but legal. Persistent non-compliance can lead to ECJ referral and financial penalties.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Offshore wind: Federal (North Sea EEZ). Onshore renewables, energy efficiency in buildings, regional grid: Regions. Heating & transport biofuels: mixed. Belgium used statistical transfers from other MS to meet 2020 target. The inter-federal Energy Pact sets the framework; regions implement under their own competences.
indicators.short_description: Share of renewables in total energy consumption — Belgium has one of the lowest rates in the EU.

indicators.description: Total primary energy consumption before processing, excluding exports, marine bunkers and non-energy uses.
indicators.target_context: EU indicative target; Belgium's own NECP target is less ambitious at 1.52 EJ
indicators.policy: EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) — Directive (EU) 2023/1791
indicators.data_source_description: Annual primary energy consumption 2005–2022 in exajoules | Belgian macroeconomic and energy modelling data
indicators.notes: 2022 value = 1.89 EJ (≈45.1 Mtoe). Target = 1.41 EJ (≈33.7 Mtoe). Current trajectory does not meet either EU or NECP target.
indicators.consequences: The EED target is indicative (not strictly binding for member states individually). However, failure to implement required energy efficiency measures can trigger infringement proceedings. No direct financial penalty per unit of energy over-consumed, but infringement → ECJ → lump sum / daily fines. Belgium already missed its 2020 indicative targets.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Energy efficiency in buildings, industry and transport: primarily Regional. Product standards, building codes at federal level. No single authority is solely responsible — requires coordination via ICE.
indicators.short_description: Total primary energy use before processing — Belgium is off track for both its EU and national targets.

indicators.description: Total final energy consumed by end users (industry, transport, households, services).
indicators.target_context: Climate-corrected value
indicators.policy: EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) — Directive (EU) 2023/1791
indicators.data_source_description: Final energy consumed by all end-use sectors 2022, climate-corrected | Commercial energy data by country | EU energy balance statistics
indicators.notes: EEA assessment suggests Belgium is broadly on track. Main challenges: buildings and transport sectors.
indicators.consequences: Same as Primary Energy Consumption — indicative target under EED. Infringement risk if Belgium fails to implement the required legislative and policy measures. EEA currently assesses Belgium as broadly on track, reducing immediate legal risk.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Same division as primary energy. Regions lead on buildings, industry efficiency and transport. Federal government on federal buildings and product policies.
indicators.short_description: Total energy consumed by industry, transport, households and services — currently near its 2030 target.

indicators.description: Share of gross electricity generation from renewable sources (wind, solar, hydro, biomass).
indicators.target_context: Sub-target under RED III
indicators.policy: EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED III) — Directive (EU) 2023/2413
indicators.data_source_description: Annual renewable electricity generation share by source (wind, solar, biomass, hydro) | EU renewable energy shares by technology
indicators.notes: Strong growth in offshore wind and solar PV. 2023 breakdown: wind 18%, solar 9%, biomass 5%, hydro <1%.
indicators.consequences: Sub-target under RED III. Covered by the same infringement risk as the overall RES target. No standalone fine mechanism for this sub-target specifically — enforced as part of overall RED compliance.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Offshore wind: Federal. Onshore wind, solar PV, biomass: Regional. Grid infrastructure and market regulation: partly Federal (transmission) and Regional (distribution). Belgium's nuclear policy (lifetime extension) is a Federal decision impacting the electricity mix.
indicators.short_description: Share of electricity generated from wind, solar, hydro and biomass — growing strongly since 2020.

indicators.description: Net-zero greenhouse gas emissions across the entire economy.
indicators.target_context: Net-zero — no numerical interim target set at national level
indicators.policy: European Climate Law — Reg. (EU) 2021/1119; Paris Agreement (Art. 4)
indicators.data_source_description: EU climate neutrality assessments and long-term strategy documents | Belgian macroeconomic and energy modelling
indicators.notes: Belgium's LTS2050 supports the pathway but lacks a binding national neutrality target. Court ruling Nov 2023 found current targets legally insufficient.
indicators.consequences: Under the European Climate Law, net-zero by 2050 is a binding EU-level obligation. The Commission can open infringement proceedings against member states that fail to adopt adequate policies. No direct financial fine specified, but ECJ referral is possible. Belgium's LTS2050 does not yet include a binding national 2050 target, leaving it exposed.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. All levels must contribute. No binding national law currently assigns 2050 targets by level of government. The three regions each have 2050 visions in their own climate plans. Federal level adopted a Long-Term Strategy (LTS2050) but without binding national neutrality commitment.
indicators.short_description: Belgium's pathway to net-zero emissions by 2050 — no binding national target currently in place.

indicators.description: Average annual GHG emissions per Belgian inhabitant (territorial, net incl. LULUCF).
indicators.target_context: Paris Agreement 1.5°C fair-share level (equal per-capita global budget)
indicators.policy: Paris Agreement (Art. 2 & 4); IPCC SR1.5 (2018)
indicators.data_source_description: Per-capita GHG derived from national GHG inventory | Carbon inequality and Paris fair-share budget research | Consumption-based emissions research
indicators.notes: Belgium (8.3 t) is 3.6× the 2030 Paris fair-share level (2.3 t). Consumption-based footprint (incl. imports) ~12–14 t/cap. Some equity frameworks argue wealthy nations should go further given historical emissions.
indicators.consequences: No direct financial penalty for missing a per-capita target — this is a derived indicator rather than a legally binding standalone obligation. Consequences flow through the underlying regulations (ESR, Climate Law) rather than this metric directly. Used for political accountability and public communication.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Same division as total GHG emissions — per-capita is a derived indicator from the national total, so responsibility mirrors the overall GHG governance structure.
indicators.short_description: Average annual emissions per Belgian — at 8.3 t/cap, more than 3× the Paris Agreement fair-share level.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian land area legally designated as protected (Natura 2000 + nationally designated areas combined).
indicators.target_context: EU 30x30 target; Belgium at 14.7% — needs to more than double, requiring a 47-fold increase in the annual designation rate
indicators.policy: EU Biodiversity Strategy for 2030 — COM(2020) 380; Kunming-Montréal GBF Target 3
indicators.notes: Natura 2000 covers 12.7% of land; nationally designated areas add 5.4% (most overlapping with Natura 2000). Share almost unchanged since 2012. EU average: 26.4% in 2023.
indicators.consequences: EU infringement proceedings if Belgium fails to designate sufficient protected areas or implement adequate management. The EU Biodiversity Strategy is not itself directly enforceable as a regulation, but the underlying Habitats and Birds Directives are binding. Belgium has previously faced infringement for inadequate Natura 2000 implementation. No direct financial fine per % of land unprotected, but ECJ referral and fines are possible for systematic non-compliance with the Habitats Directive.
indicators.responsible: Primarily Regional (3 Regions). Nature conservation and protected area designation are core regional competences. Flanders: ANB (Agency for Nature and Forests). Wallonia: DNF (Department of Nature and Forests). Brussels: Bruxelles Environnement. Federal government: responsible for marine protected areas (North Sea) and Natura 2000 on federal domains (military, railway). All coordinated through ICE.
indicators.short_description: Share of Belgium's land legally protected — at 14.7%, well below the EU 30% target for 2030.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian land under strict protection — areas where no or minimal human intervention is permitted, to preserve highest-value biodiversity.
indicators.target_context: At least one-third of the 30% protected area target must be strictly protected (10% of total land)
indicators.policy: EU Biodiversity Strategy for 2030 — COM(2020) 380
indicators.notes: Belgium has not yet formally defined or mapped its strictly protected areas. Definition and criteria under development at EU level. Current value not reported.
indicators.consequences: Same infringement risk as protected land area overall. Currently low immediate legal risk as the definition and criteria for 'strictly protected' areas are still being developed at EU level. Enforcement likely to increase once criteria are formally agreed.
indicators.responsible: Primarily Regional (3 Regions). Same as protected land area. Federal role limited to marine areas and federal estates.
indicators.short_description: Share of land under strict protection with minimal human intervention — definition still being finalised.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian marine waters (North Sea EEZ) legally designated as protected under Natura 2000 and national designations.
indicators.target_context: Already exceeds 30% target — one of few Belgian biodiversity goals achieved
indicators.policy: EU Biodiversity Strategy for 2030 — COM(2020) 380; OSPAR Convention
indicators.notes: Belgium's North Sea EEZ is relatively small (~3,600 km²). Marine protection coverage is high (37.8%) but effectiveness of management is debated — designation alone does not guarantee biodiversity recovery.
indicators.consequences: Target already achieved (37.8%). No current legal risk. However, OSPAR Convention and Habitats Directive require effective management of designated sites — designation alone is not sufficient. Infringement risk if sites are not effectively managed.
indicators.responsible: Federal. The North Sea EEZ falls under federal competence. Managed by the Marine Environment Service (FPS Health). Offshore wind, marine Natura 2000 and OSPAR commitments are all federal responsibilities.
indicators.short_description: Share of Belgium's North Sea waters legally protected — already exceeding the 30% EU target.

indicators.indicator: Organic Farming Share (% of agricultural area)
indicators.description: Share of total utilised agricultural area (UAA) managed under certified organic farming.
indicators.target_context: EU Farm to Fork target; Belgium well below at 7.9% — needs to more than triple
indicators.policy: EU Farm to Fork Strategy / EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 — COM(2020) 381
indicators.notes: Belgium had 7.9% organic area in 2022, up from ~4% in 2012. Growth is positive but far below the 25% target. EU average was 10.5% in 2022. Flanders lags Wallonia significantly.
indicators.consequences: No direct penalty for missing the 25% organic target — it is set in the Farm to Fork Strategy (a policy communication, not a binding regulation). However, CAP Strategic Plans must include organic farming support measures; failure to implement CAP correctly can trigger financial corrections (clawback of EU agricultural subsidies).
indicators.responsible: Primarily Regional (3 Regions). Agricultural policy and CAP implementation are Regional competences. Each region has its own organic farming support scheme. Coordination via inter-federal agricultural bodies. Federal: product labelling and fraud control.
indicators.short_description: Share of agricultural land under certified organic farming — at 7.9%, far below the 25% EU target.

indicators.indicator: Species & Habitats in Favourable Conservation Status
indicators.description: Share of species and habitats assessed under the EU Habitats and Birds Directives that are in favourable conservation status or showing a positive trend, among those currently in unfavourable status.
indicators.target_context: At least 30% of those currently in unfavourable status must reach favourable or show strong positive trend by 2030
indicators.policy: EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 / EU Habitats Directive (92/43/EEC) / Birds Directive (2009/147/EC)
indicators.notes: Latest Habitats Directive assessment (Art. 17, 2019): only 4.3% of Belgian habitats of European importance in favourable conservation status. Situation is very poor — one of the worst in the EU for habitats. Reported every 6 years; next report due 2025.
indicators.consequences: Binding under the Habitats Directive (Art. 17). Belgium must report every 6 years. Failure to achieve or maintain favourable conservation status, or to take adequate conservation measures, can trigger infringement proceedings → ECJ → fines. Belgium has already received formal notices regarding inadequate Natura 2000 management. Conservation status in Belgium is among the worst in EU.
indicators.responsible: Primarily Regional (3 Regions). Habitats Directive reporting and conservation management are regional. Flanders reports via INBO; Wallonia via DEMNA; Brussels via Bruxelles Environnement. Federal level coordinates the consolidated national submission to the EU every 6 years.
indicators.short_description: Share of EU-protected species and habitats in good condition — Belgium is among the worst in Europe.

indicators.description: The farmland bird population index tracks populations of birds dependent on agricultural landscapes — the best available annual proxy for overall pollinator and biodiversity health in agricultural areas.
indicators.target_context: Halt and reverse decline — no specific numeric target set for Belgium; 1990 baseline used as reference for reversal
indicators.policy: EU Nature Restoration Regulation (Art. 11) / EU Pollinators Initiative (revised 2023)
indicators.data_source: EEA / Research Institute for Nature and Forest (INBO)
indicators.notes: Farmland bird index fell from 100 (1990) to 50.9 (2023) — a catastrophic 49% decline. No numeric recovery target set for Belgium specifically. NRR requires halt and reversal by 2030. EU standardised pollinator monitoring scheme to be deployed by 2026. The grassland butterfly index is an additional indicator (data less complete).
indicators.consequences: Legally binding under Nature Restoration Regulation (Art. 11) — Belgium must halt and reverse pollinator decline by 2030 and deploy the EU pollinator monitoring scheme by 2026. Non-compliance → infringement → ECJ → fines. The NRR is the first binding legal instrument on pollinators.
indicators.responsible: Primarily Regional (3 Regions). Agricultural practices impacting pollinators: Regional (CAP, agri-environment). Urban green spaces: Regional/municipal. Pesticide authorisation: Federal. Monitoring coordination: Federal (via National Focal Points) + Regional (INBO, Natagora, SPARNE-DEMNA).
indicators.short_description: Index tracking farmland bird populations — a proxy for broader biodiversity health in agricultural areas.

indicators.indicator: Invasive Alien Species Threatening Red-Listed Species
indicators.description: Reduction in the number of red-listed species threatened by invasive alien species (IAS), as a proportion of those currently threatened.
indicators.target_context: Halve the number of red-listed species threatened by IAS vs current baseline
indicators.policy: EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 / EU IAS Regulation (1143/2014) / Kunming-Montréal GBF Target 6
indicators.notes: IAS are a growing pressure on Belgian biodiversity. The EU IAS Regulation lists species of Union concern requiring management. No Belgium-specific baseline percentage currently published. Trend is worsening as new IAS (e.g. Asian hornet, Fallopia japonica) continue to spread.
indicators.consequences: Binding under EU IAS Regulation (1143/2014) — member states must manage listed species of Union concern. Failure to implement required management measures can trigger infringement. No specific penalty for the -50% red-listed species target (part of Biodiversity Strategy, not IAS Regulation directly).
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Federal: trade and import controls (CITES, customs), marine IAS. Regions: terrestrial management of IAS on regional territory. Coordination via the Interministerial Conference on the Environment. Belgium has a National IAS Committee.
indicators.short_description: Number of red-listed species threatened by invasive alien species — currently insufficient data.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian terrestrial ecosystem area where atmospheric nitrogen deposition exceeds the critical load — the threshold above which significant biodiversity loss occurs. Driven mainly by NH3 from agriculture.
indicators.latest_value: ~14 (EU-27 proxy; Belgium likely lower — see notes)
indicators.target_context: ZPAP Target 3: reduce EU ecosystem area exceeding N critical loads for eutrophication by 25% vs 2005. 2005 baseline: ~86% of EU ecosystem area exceeded critical loads. The 25% reduction target means reaching ~64% by 2030. Long-term goal (Gothenburg Protocol): 0% exceedance. Belgium is a chronic hotspot alongside the Netherlands and northern Germany — intensity of exceedance (eq N ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹) remains very high even where area is shrinking.
indicators.policy: EU Zero Pollution Action Plan / NEC Directive (2016/2284) / EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030
indicators.data_source: EEA indicator: Eutrophication caused by atmospheric nitrogen deposition in Europe. GIS data: EMEP MSC-W & CCE (Umweltbundesamt). Belgium NEC emissions: IRCELINE/VMM/Bruxelles Environnement.
indicators.notes:
HOW THE INDICATOR WORKS:
Two metrics exist: (1) % of ecosystem area exceeding critical load [used for ZPAP target]; (2) Average Accumulated Exceedance (AAE) in eq N ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹ [measures intensity of exceedance].

WHAT 'CRITICAL LOAD' MEANS: The maximum nitrogen deposition an ecosystem can absorb without long-term damage. When exceeded, eutrophication occurs — excess nitrogen favours aggressive plant species, reducing biodiversity.

BELGIUM-SPECIFIC DATA AVAILABILITY: No Belgium country-level % figure is published in a national table. Data exists only in the EEA/CCE GIS raster dataset (grid-cell level). The EU-27 figure (14% reduction by 2023) is used as proxy. Belgium almost certainly performs below this average given its persistent hotspot status — the border area with the Netherlands remains among the most severely affected in all of Europe.

PROXY — BELGIUM NH3 EMISSIONS: Belgium met its 2030 NEC commitment for NH3 already in 2020 (one of only 2 MS to do so). This is positive, but NH3 reduction alone does not translate immediately to critical load compliance, as accumulated nitrogen in soils persists for decades.

EU-WIDE BASELINE SCENARIO: In 2005, ~86% of EU ecosystem area exceeded N critical loads. By 2023: ~75% (14% reduction). 2030 target: ~64% (25% reduction). The EC 4th Clean Air Outlook projects this is 'unlikely' to be achieved under current policies.
indicators.consequences: Indirect legal consequences via NEC Directive (binding NH3 and NOx ceilings) and potentially Water Framework Directive (if eutrophication leads to water quality failures). Belgium has previously missed NEC ceilings for NH3. Direct infringement possible for missing NEC ceilings → ECJ → fines. The 25% reduction target (ZPAP) is a policy target without standalone legal enforceability.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. NH3 from agriculture: Regional (agricultural policy). NOx from transport: Regional (mobility policy) and Federal (product standards, fuel). Industrial emissions: Regional (permitting) and Federal (product policy). NEC Directive compliance is a national obligation coordinated federally but delivered by regions.
indicators.short_description: Share of ecosystems where nitrogen deposition exceeds safe limits — mainly driven by agriculture.

indicators.description: Share of municipal solid waste (incl. composting/fermentation) recycled or composted in Belgium. Harmonised EU definition applies from 2020 onwards (incl. commercial & office waste sources).
indicators.target_context: 55% by 2025, 60% by 2030 (EU Waste Framework Directive 2018/851). Belgium at 56% in 2023 — above the 2025 threshold.
indicators.policy: EU Waste Framework Directive — Directive (EU) 2018/851
indicators.notes: Belgium's 2023 figure (56%) includes material recycling (34%) and composting/fermentation (22%). Series break in 2020 due to EU harmonisation of municipal waste definition; comparisons with pre-2020 figures require caution. Achieving 60% by 2030 is ambitious but feasible given current trajectory. EEA 2022 early warning did not flag Belgium as at risk.
indicators.consequences: Failure to meet 2025 and 2030 targets triggers EU infringement proceedings under Art. 11b WFD. Early warning reports published 3 years before deadlines. Persistent shortfall → ECJ referral → financial fines.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Flanders: OVAM. Wallonia: SPW / AWaP. Brussels: Bruxelles-Environnement. Federal coordinates national data submission to Eurostat.
indicators.short_description: Share of household and commercial waste recycled or composted — Belgium is above the 2025 EU target.

indicators.description: Share of total packaging waste (all materials: paper/cardboard, glass, plastic, metal, wood) placed on the Belgian market that is recycled. Governed by the Interregional Cooperation Agreement on Packaging Waste.
indicators.target_context: 65% by 2025, 70% by 2030 (Directive 94/62/EC as amended). Belgium at 79.7% — already exceeds the 2030 target.
indicators.policy: Packaging and Packaging Waste Directive — Directive 94/62/EC as amended; Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (EU) 2025/40
indicators.notes: 2023 breakdown by material: glass 93%, metals 97%, paper/cardboard 84%, plastic 60%, wood 66%. Paper/cardboard = 40% of total packaging weight. Belgium is one of 7 EU countries already meeting the 2030 target. The new PPWR (EU 2025/40) introduces binding waste prevention targets (−5% by 2030 vs 2018) applicable from August 2026.
indicators.consequences: Belgium already exceeds the 2030 target (70%). The PPWR introduces prevention and recycled content requirements. Non-compliance with the directly applicable Regulation from August 2026 → infringement risk if national law not updated in time.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal framework (Interregional Cooperation Agreement; IVCIE). Regional implementation: OVAM (Flanders), SPW (Wallonia), Bruxelles-Environnement (Brussels). Fost Plus = household packaging EPR; Valipac = industrial/commercial packaging EPR.
indicators.short_description: Share of all packaging materials recycled — Belgium already exceeds the 2030 EU target of 70%.

indicators.description: Share of plastic packaging waste recycled in Belgium. Plastic is the most challenging material due to polymer diversity and contamination, yet Belgium leads the EU.
indicators.target_context: EU target: 55% of plastic packaging recycled by 2030 (Directive 94/62/EC). Belgium at 59.5% — highest rate in the EU.
indicators.policy: Packaging and Packaging Waste Directive — Directive 94/62/EC; Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (EU) 2025/40
indicators.notes: Belgium ranked #1 in the EU for plastic packaging recycling in 2023 (59.5%), up from 39% in 2013. EU average was 42.1% in 2023. Plastic packaging = 18% of total packaging weight in Belgium. PPWR (EU 2025/40) introduces recycled content targets (30% recycled plastic in all plastic bottles by 2030) and design-for-recycling requirements applicable from August 2026.
indicators.consequences: Target already exceeded. PPWR will introduce recycled content targets and stricter labelling requirements. Non-compliance with PPWR from August 2026 → infringement risk.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal cooperation agreement (IVCIE). Regional implementation via OVAM (Flanders), SPW (Wallonia), Bruxelles-Environnement. EPR schemes (Fost Plus / Valipac) are the main operational vehicles.
indicators.short_description: Share of plastic packaging recycled — Belgium ranks first in the EU at 59.5%, above the 2030 target.

indicators.description: Annual quantity of municipal waste (household + similar commercial/office waste) generated per Belgian inhabitant. No binding EU kg/capita target exists; trend reduction is an SDG and EU prevention programme objective.
indicators.target_context: No binding EU kg/capita target. EU-27 average (~513 kg/cap in 2022) used as aspirational benchmark. Belgium is 37% above the EU average. EU SDG 12.5 requires substantial reduction by 2030.
indicators.policy: EU Waste Framework Directive — Directive (EU) 2018/851 (waste prevention obligations); EU SDG 12.5
indicators.notes: Belgium generated 700 kg/cap in 2023, up from 683 kg in 2022 (but below 760 kg peak in 2021). The EU27 average was ~513 kg/cap in 2022. The 2020 series break (inclusion of commercial/office waste) inflated the figure. The PPWR introduces binding packaging waste prevention targets (−5% by 2030 vs 2018); no equivalent binding kg/cap target exists for total municipal waste. Target value in this row uses EU27 average as benchmark — not a legally binding number.
indicators.consequences: No direct financial penalty for per-capita generation — trend indicator, not a standalone binding target. Consequences flow through waste prevention programme obligations under WFD Art. 29: failure to implement adequate prevention programmes can trigger infringement.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Each region has its own Waste Prevention Programme: Flanders (Lokaal Materialenplan 2023–2030 / OVAM), Wallonia (Plan wallon des Déchets-Ressources / SPW), Brussels (Plan de Gestion des Ressources et des Déchets). Federal coordinates national reporting.
indicators.short_description: Annual waste generated per person — at 700 kg, Belgium is 37% above the EU average.

indicators.description: Share of municipal waste landfilled. Belgium has near-zero landfilling due to region-wide landfill bans and high landfill taxes — among the best performers in the EU.
indicators.target_context: EU target: max 10% landfilled by 2035 (Landfill Directive 1999/31/EC as amended). Belgium at 0.1% — target effectively achieved a decade ahead of schedule.
indicators.policy: EU Landfill Directive — Directive 1999/31/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2018/850
indicators.notes: Belgium's 2023 landfill rate (0.1%) is among the lowest in the EU. Flanders has banned landfilling of separately collected waste (1998), combustible waste TOC>6% (2000) and biodegradable waste (2007). Landfill tax = EUR 107.87/tonne combustible in Flanders; EUR 119.59/tonne in Wallonia — among highest in EU. Brussels has no landfill facility. High incineration rate (~44% in 2023) is the main destination for non-recyclable waste — a policy tension with circular economy ambitions.
indicators.consequences: Target far exceeded. No compliance risk. Formal early warning report due in 2032. Note: EEA 2023 data not collected under the formal compliance methodology; official compliance assessments may differ slightly.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Landfill bans and taxes are set by regional governments: OVAM (Flanders), SPW/AWaP (Wallonia), Bruxelles-Environnement (Brussels). Brussels has no landfill in its territory.
indicators.short_description: Share of waste sent to landfill — Belgium is a top EU performer at just 0.1%, well below the target.

indicators.description: Share of total material inputs in the Belgian economy accounted for by recycled materials fed back into the economy (secondary materials). Also called the circularity rate. Eurostat indicator CEI_SRM030.
indicators.target_context: EU target: double CMUR to ~24% by 2030 (Clean Industrial Deal 2025 KPI; EU Circular Economy Action Plan). Belgium at 22.2% in 2022 — 2nd highest in EU (after Netherlands at 27.5%), already close to the EU 2030 target.
indicators.policy: EU Circular Economy Action Plan (2020) / Clean Industrial Deal (2025) / 8th Environment Action Programme
indicators.notes: Belgium's CMUR rose from 13.6% in 2010 to 22.2% in 2022 (+8.6 pp). The EU27 CMUR was ~11.8% in 2023. The 24% CMUR target is an EU-level policy objective in the Clean Industrial Deal, not a binding legal obligation for individual member states. Data is updated annually but with a 2-year lag for actual waste statistics; odd years are Eurostat estimates.
indicators.consequences: No binding standalone legal obligation for Belgium to meet a specific CMUR figure. The underlying recycling and waste directives (which drive CMUR) are binding — failure to implement them can trigger infringement proceedings.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. CMUR is a national-level aggregate indicator. Regional governments primarily responsible for waste management and recycling policies driving CMUR. Federal responsible for product standards, chemicals policy and EPR framework legislation.
indicators.short_description: Share of recycled materials in total economic inputs — Belgium is 2nd in the EU at 22.2%.

indicators.indicator: Surface Waters in Good Ecological Status
indicators.description: Share of Belgian surface water bodies (rivers, lakes, transitional and coastal waters) achieving good or high ecological status or ecological potential, as defined by the EU Water Framework Directive.
indicators.target_context: 100% of all water bodies in good status by 2027 (WFD Art. 4). Belgium at 27.4% — well below EU average of 39.5%. No Belgian surface water body meets good chemical status (0% in 2021 vs 2.2% in 2015), mainly due to PFAS, mercury and heavy metals.
indicators.policy: EU Water Framework Directive — Directive 2000/60/EC
indicators.data_source: EEA State of Water 2024 / EC 3rd RBMP Assessment 2025 / WISE Freshwater
indicators.notes: Belgium reported under its 3rd River Basin Management Plans (2022–2027). Ecological status based on biological, physicochemical and hydromorphological quality elements using the "one out, all out" principle — one failing parameter means the whole water body fails. Main pressures: nitrates and pesticides from agriculture (especially Flanders), wastewater discharges, and hydromorphological alterations. The 0% good chemical status is driven by ubiquitous substances (PFAS, mercury, brominated flame retardants); if these are excluded, significantly more water bodies would pass. Belgium has not invoked Art. 4(5) WFD to lower objectives, leaving it exposed to infringement. EU average: 39.5% good ecological status in 2021. Over 90% of surface waters in Belgium's river basin districts fall below good status.
indicators.consequences: WFD is a binding directive. Failure to achieve good status by 2027 triggers EU infringement proceedings. Belgium already faces European Commission scrutiny (EC country-specific report published Feb 2025). Infringement → ECJ referral → lump sum and/or periodic fines. EC Commissioner Roswall warned Belgium in February 2025 to "move quickly." Belgium also risks financial sanctions under the EU Water Resilience Strategy.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Water policy is a regional competence. Flanders: VMM (Flemish Environment Agency) and Departement Omgeving. Wallonia: SPW / Direction des Eaux de Surface. Brussels: Bruxelles-Environnement. Federal: limited role (North Sea, international coordination). Coordination via the CIW (Coördinatiecommissie Integraal Waterbeleid) and its Walloon/Brussels equivalents.
indicators.short_description: Share of rivers and lakes in good ecological condition — Belgium at 27.4%, well below the EU target.

indicators.indicator: Nitrate Pollution — Groundwater Stations Exceeding 50 mg/L
indicators.description: Share of groundwater monitoring stations in Belgium with nitrate concentrations exceeding the EU maximum allowable concentration of 50 mg NO₃/L. Belgium is among the worst performers in the EU, driven by intensive agriculture — particularly in Flanders.
indicators.target_context: EU standard: 0% of monitoring stations should exceed 50 mg NO₃/L (Nitrates Directive 91/676/EEC; Groundwater Directive 2006/118/EC). Latest confirmed value (proxy, amber): >15% of Belgian groundwater monitoring stations exceeded 50 mg NO₃/L in the 2016–2019 Nitrates Directive reporting period — placing Belgium among the 7 worst EU countries. 2020–2023 reporting cycle data are currently being compiled by Member States; no consolidated national figure published as of March 2026. Entire Flemish territory designated as Nitrate Vulnerable Zone. Flanders' derogation (2019/1205) expired Dec 2022 and was not renewed.
indicators.policy: EU Nitrates Directive — Directive 91/676/EEC; EU Groundwater Directive — Directive 2006/118/EC; Water Framework Directive
indicators.data_source: EEA 8th EAP indicator — Nitrate in groundwater / Nitrates Directive 4-yearly reporting / VMM (Flanders) / SPW (Wallonia)
indicators.notes: Latest value (15%, amber) is a proxy: it represents the share of Belgian groundwater monitoring stations exceeding 50 mg NO₃/L during the 2016–2019 Nitrates Directive reporting period (the most recent consolidated EU-level data). The actual figure was reported as ">15%" — the threshold Belgium crossed to be classified among the 7 worst EU countries in that period. The true figure is likely higher. 2020–2023 Nitrates Directive data are currently being compiled; no confirmed national figure published as of March 2026. "Off track" status is based on this 2016–2019 proxy and the structural nature of the problem: the entire Flemish territory remains a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone, Flanders' derogation was not renewed after Dec 2022, and no significant improvement in groundwater nitrate concentrations was recorded across the EU in the 2007–2023 period (EEA 8th EAP indicator). Zero Pollution Action Plan target: −50% nutrient losses to water by 2030 (EU-wide). Separate from the Nitrates Directive limit, WFD ecological thresholds for nitrates in surface water can be 5–25× lower than 50 mg/L for sensitive water types.
indicators.consequences: Nitrates Directive is binding (Directive 91/676/EEC). Belgium was subject to infringement for failing to designate sufficient NVZs and for inadequate action programmes. The non-renewal of Flanders' derogation significantly tightens obligations. Failure to meet nitrate standards → infringement → ECJ → fines. Ongoing pressure also from WFD (nitrates drive failure to achieve good ecological and chemical status).
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Flanders: VITO/VMM (monitoring), VLM (manure policy, Mestbank). Wallonia: SPW / Direction des Eaux souterraines; action programmes under the Plan de Gestion Durable de l'Azote (PGDA). Brussels: Bruxelles-Environnement. Federal: Nitrates Directive reporting coordination, CAP co-financing.
indicators.short_description: Share of groundwater stations above the nitrate safety limit — Belgium is among the worst in the EU.

indicators.description: Share of the Belgian population supplied with drinking water meeting all EU parametric values under the Drinking Water Directive. Belgium has high overall compliance but faces emerging challenges with PFAS and lead in older pipes.
indicators.target_context: EU Drinking Water Directive (2020/2184) requires compliance with all parametric values. From 1 Jan 2026: PFAS total ≤100 ng/L. Belgium has very high overall compliance (>99%) for traditional parameters. PFAS monitoring underway; results vary by region and catchment. Transposition deadline: 12 January 2023.
indicators.policy: EU Drinking Water Directive (recast) — Directive (EU) 2020/2184
indicators.data_source: Statbel / Regional water utilities (De Watergroep, Vivaqua, SWDE) / European Commission DWD reporting
indicators.notes: Belgium's three regions each transpose the DWD independently. Overall drinking water compliance with traditional parameters (microbiological, nitrates, heavy metals) is very high (>99% population served). Key emerging concerns: (1) PFAS — new limit of 100 ng/L total PFAS applies from 1 Jan 2026; Belgian catchments show variable PFAS levels, particularly near industrial sites in Flanders (Zwijndrecht, Genk area). VIVAQUA (Brussels) reports levels below the 2026 standard as of early 2022. (2) Lead — old lead pipes remain in some homes; pipe replacement programmes ongoing but incomplete. Latest value (99.5%) is a proxy estimate based on published compliance data for traditional parameters — amber fill applied as the figure is not formally published as a single national compliance rate under the new DWD. The formal DWD compliance reporting cycle to the Commission runs every 3 years.
indicators.consequences: Binding under Directive (EU) 2020/2184. Failure to transpose or comply → infringement. If PFAS levels exceed 100 ng/L from January 2026, water suppliers must take remedial action or restrict supply. Non-compliance with DWD can trigger infringement proceedings against the Member State. The PFAS 2026 threshold is a particular risk for some Flemish catchments.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Drinking water supply is a regional competence. Flanders: De Watergroep, Farys, Water-link (regulated by VREG). Wallonia: SWDE, inter-communales (regulated by CWaPE-water). Brussels: Vivaqua / IBDE. Federal government: very limited role (some product standards). Each region has its own regulatory authority.
indicators.short_description: Share of Belgians supplied with safe drinking water — compliance is high but PFAS poses new risks.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian groundwater body area in good chemical status, assessed under the EU Water Framework Directive. Main pollutants causing failure: nitrates, pesticides, PFAS. Assessed every 6 years in River Basin Management Plans.
indicators.target_context: 100% of groundwater bodies in good chemical status by 2027 (WFD Art. 4). Belgium at 53.1% in 2021 (3rd RBMP), improved from 41.3% in 2015 (2nd RBMP). EU average: ~75% in good chemical status. Quantitative status: 87.7% in 2021 (slight decline of 0.3% vs 2015), with poor quantitative status concentrated in Flanders.
indicators.policy: EU Water Framework Directive — Directive 2000/60/EC; EU Groundwater Directive — Directive 2006/118/EC
indicators.data_source: EC 3rd RBMP Assessment 2025 / EEA State of Water 2024 / WISE Freshwater WFD database
indicators.notes: Belgium's groundwater chemical status has improved substantially: from 41.3% (2015) to 53.1% (2021), a gain of ~12 percentage points. However, ~47% of groundwater body area still fails good chemical status — well below the EU average of ~75%. Main pollutants causing failure: nitrates (from agriculture), pesticides, and PFAS (industrial contamination, especially in Flanders). Quantitative status (groundwater level and flow): 87.7% of area in good status in 2021, a marginal decline of 0.3% vs 2015; most poor quantitative bodies are in Flanders, linked to overextraction. Note: groundwater chemical status is assessed using the "one out, all out" principle — one failing pollutant renders the whole body "poor". The next assessment cycle (4th RBMP) covers 2022–2027 and will be reported in 2028.
indicators.consequences: WFD is binding. Failure to achieve good groundwater status by 2027 → infringement. Belgium has already been cited in EU-level assessments as failing to meet WFD objectives for both surface and groundwater. PFAS contamination in Flemish groundwater is subject to ongoing enforcement actions nationally (particularly around 3M Zwijndrecht site). ECJ referral possible for persistent non-compliance.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Flanders: VMM (monitoring), VLM (agriculture/nitrates), OVAM (contaminated sites including PFAS). Wallonia: SPW / Direction des Eaux souterraines. Brussels: Bruxelles-Environnement. Federal: product legislation (pesticides, chemicals), PFAS phase-out under EU REACH.
indicators.short_description: Share of groundwater in good chemical condition — improving but still 47% of area below target.

indicators.description: Share of Belgian land area covered by impervious surfaces — roads, buildings, parking lots and other hard infrastructure — rendering soil impermeable to water and destroying its ecological functions. Belgium has one of the highest rates in the EU, reflecting high population density and historically dispersed ribbon development.
indicators.target_context: No specific % ceiling exists as an EU legal target. EU policy direction: net land take = 0 by 2050 (EU Soil Strategy 2021), meaning no additional net sealing from that point onwards — not that existing sealed land must be removed. Flanders has a stricter regional target: net land take = 0 by 2040 (bouwshift). Belgium's ~13.7% rate is among the 4–5 highest in the EU (EU average ~2.7%). The indicator is tracked as a trend: is the rate of new sealing slowing?
indicators.policy: EU Soil Strategy for 2030 — COM(2021) 699; EU Biodiversity Strategy for 2030; proposed EU Soil Monitoring Law (SML)
indicators.data_source: Copernicus Land Monitoring Service — High Resolution Layer Imperviousness / EEA soil sealing indicator
indicators.notes: 13.7% means approximately 13.7% of Belgium's total land surface (~4,200 km² out of 30,530 km²) is covered by roads, buildings, parking and other hard impervious infrastructure. This is one of the highest shares in the EU, where the average is ~2.7% (2018). The high rate reflects Belgium's high population density (376/km²) and decades of dispersed ribbon development along roads, particularly in Flanders. Note: the 2021 Copernicus figure (~13.7%) is not directly comparable to pre-2018 data due to the upgrade from 20m to 10m spatial resolution, which captures more fine-grained sealed surfaces. The 2006 harmonised figure (100m resolution) was ~10%. EU Soil Monitoring Law (proposed 2023, not yet adopted) will introduce binding soil health targets including sealing-related objectives. Flanders' bouwshift policy aims to reduce the rate of new land take to zero by 2040, which is more ambitious than the EU 2050 target.
indicators.consequences: Currently policy targets (Soil Strategy, Biodiversity Strategy) rather than binding legal obligations. The proposed EU Soil Monitoring Law (SML), once adopted, will introduce binding soil health objectives with potential infringement implications. Belgium's Flemish Spatial Planning Strategy ("bouwshift") has binding regional targets — failure to implement regional land use plans could trigger domestic legal challenges. No direct EU financial penalty currently for sealing rates.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Land use planning is an exclusive regional competence. Flanders: Departement Omgeving (bouwshift policy — aiming for net land take = 0 by 2040). Wallonia: SPW / DGO4 (Aménagement du territoire). Brussels: Perspective.Brussels. Federal: very limited role in land use planning.
indicators.short_description: Share of land covered by impervious surfaces — Belgium has one of the highest rates in the EU.

indicators.description: Annual mean concentration of fine particulate matter (diameter ≤2.5 µm) in ambient air, weighted by population. PM2.5 is the single largest environmental health risk in Belgium, linked to cardiovascular and respiratory disease and premature death.
indicators.target_context: New EU annual limit value: 10 µg/m³ by 2030 (Directive 2024/2881, halved from 25 µg/m³). Current (2023) EU limit: 25 µg/m³ — met across Belgium. Population-weighted mean 8.5 µg/m³ in 2023 is near but above the new 2030 limit. WHO guideline: 5 µg/m³ (not met anywhere in Belgium — 88.7% of population lives in areas exceeding it).
indicators.policy: EU Ambient Air Quality Directive (recast) — Directive (EU) 2024/2881
indicators.data_source: IRCEL-CELINE — RIO interpolation / EEA Air Quality e-Reporting
indicators.notes: Population-weighted mean: 8.5 µg/m³ in 2023 (IRCEL, RIO 4×4 km grid). Regional breakdown 2023: Flanders 9.5, Brussels 8.7, Wallonia 5.0 µg/m³. Concentrations have declined significantly since 2006. New 2030 EU limit (10 µg/m³) appears achievable at urban background level; traffic hotspots may require additional effort. 88.7% of Belgian population lives above the WHO annual guideline of 5 µg/m³. Wood burning (residential) is the largest primary PM2.5 source in Belgium; agriculture (NH3) is the largest precursor source. PM2.5 was responsible for >8,000 premature deaths in Belgium in 2014 (EEA estimate).
indicators.consequences: Binding under Directive (EU) 2024/2881. If limit value of 10 µg/m³ is not met by 2030, Member States must draw up an air quality roadmap by 2028 at the latest. Failure to comply → infringement proceedings → ECJ → fines. Belgium is currently on track at urban background level but at-risk at busy traffic locations.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Air quality plans and monitoring: Flanders (VMM), Wallonia (ISSeP/AWAC), Brussels (Bruxelles-Environnement). IRCEL-CELINE coordinates national monitoring. Federal: emission standards for vehicles and products, industrial permitting (some). Zero Low Emission Zones (LEZ) are a regional instrument in Brussels, Antwerp, Ghent.
indicators.short_description: Concentration of fine particles in air — the biggest environmental health risk in Belgium.

indicators.description: Annual mean concentration of nitrogen dioxide in ambient air. NO₂ is primarily traffic-related, causing respiratory inflammation and exacerbating asthma. It is also a precursor of ozone and secondary PM2.5.
indicators.target_context: New EU annual limit value: 20 µg/m³ by 2030 (Directive 2024/2881, halved from 40 µg/m³). Current limit of 40 µg/m³ met since 2014 across all Belgian grid cells. Highest 2023 grid-cell value: 25.9 µg/m³. Regional averages 2023: Flanders 9.1, Brussels 14.6, Wallonia 5.3 µg/m³. At busy traffic locations, 30–40 µg/m³ still measured — exceeding the 2030 limit. Latest value (9.1) = Flanders spatial average; Brussels is the limiting factor.
indicators.policy: EU Ambient Air Quality Directive (recast) — Directive (EU) 2024/2881
indicators.data_source: IRCEL-CELINE — RIO interpolation / EEA (Belgium had 1 station above 2030 HLV in 2023)
indicators.notes: National spatial average 2023: ~9 µg/m³ (well below the 2030 limit). However, Belgium was one of 4 EU MS with at least one station exceeding the new 2030 hourly limit value (HLV of 200 µg/m³) in 2023. At urban background stations, the 20 µg/m³ limit is achievable by 2030; at the busiest traffic locations (30–40 µg/m³), additional measures are needed. 46.2% of Belgian population lives in areas exceeding the WHO annual guideline of 10 µg/m³. Long-term declining trend driven by stricter Euro emission standards and the decline of diesel cars. NO₂ linked to >1,800 premature deaths in Belgium annually (EEA). Low Emission Zones (LEZ) in Brussels, Antwerp and Ghent are showing measurable impact.
indicators.consequences: Binding under Directive (EU) 2024/2881. If 20 µg/m³ limit not met in 2026, Belgium must produce a roadmap by 2028. Failure to comply by 2030 → infringement → ECJ → fines. Belgium is broadly on track at background level but needs extra effort at traffic hotspots.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Monitoring: VMM (Flanders), ISSeP/AWAC (Wallonia), Bruxelles-Environnement (Brussels). LEZ implementation: regional. Federal: vehicle emission standards (Euro norms), product regulation. IRCEL-CELINE: national coordination.
indicators.short_description: Concentration of nitrogen dioxide in air — mainly from traffic, on track to meet the 2030 EU limit.

indicators.description: Number of days per year on which the daily maximum 8-hour mean ozone concentration exceeds 120 µg/m³, averaged over 3 years. Ground-level ozone is formed by photochemical reactions from NOₓ and VOC precursors; unlike urban NO₂, ozone is typically worse in rural areas.
indicators.target_context: EU target value: ≤25 days/year (3-year average) exceedance of 120 µg/m³ (Directive 2024/2881). Met across Belgium in 2022–2024 period. Long-term objective (EU): 0 days exceedance (not met). WHO guideline: 100 µg/m³ — exceeded everywhere in Belgium. Latest value (18): approximate 3-year average for 2021–2023 at most stations.
indicators.policy: EU Ambient Air Quality Directive (recast) — Directive (EU) 2024/2881
indicators.data_source: IRCEL-CELINE ozone monitoring / EEA Air Quality e-Reporting
indicators.notes: The EU 25-day target value (3-year average) was met at all Belgian stations in 2022–2024 (IRCEL confirmed). However, the long-term trend is worrying: since 2016, annual mean ozone concentrations have been increasing across all Belgian regions, with the highest relative increases (+>1%/year) in Flanders around Antwerp and Ghent. Ozone forms from NOₓ and VOC precursors in sunlight — partly a transboundary problem. Counter-intuitively, ozone is highest in rural areas (NO from traffic breaks down ozone in cities). The WHO daily guideline (100 µg/m³ max 8-hour mean) is not met anywhere in Belgium. Ozone damages crops and ecosystems (see Section 3). Status is "On track" vs the EU 25-day target, but worsening trend raises concern for the long-term objective.
indicators.consequences: Target value of 25 days (3-year average) is legally binding under Directive (EU) 2024/2881. Currently met in Belgium. Failure to meet → infringement. The long-term objective (0 days) is aspirational. Ozone precursor emissions (NOₓ, VOC) are regulated under the NEC Directive; failure to meet NEC commitments → separate infringement risk.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Ozone is primarily a transboundary pollutant — Belgium cannot solve it unilaterally. Precursor reductions (NOₓ from transport, VOC from industry and solvents): regional and federal action. Monitoring: IRCEL-CELINE. Alert protocols: regional activation, IRCEL coordination.
indicators.short_description: Days per year with unsafe ozone levels — currently within EU limits but the long-term trend is rising.

indicators.description: Total national ammonia emissions from all sectors (predominantly agriculture — livestock manure and fertiliser application). NH₃ is a major precursor of secondary PM2.5 and drives ecosystem eutrophication and acidification.
indicators.target_context: NEC Directive (EU) 2016/2284: Belgium must achieve −5% NH₃ vs 2005 by 2020–2029 and −24% by 2030. Belgium is one of only 2 EU MS already meeting its 2030 NH₃ commitment (EEA 2024 briefing, based on 2022 data). Belgium's 2005 baseline NH₃: ~107 kt. 2022 emissions approximately 81 kt — a ~24% reduction. A notable achievement, primarily driven by Flemish manure policy (MAP6/MAP7).
indicators.policy: EU NEC Directive — Directive (EU) 2016/2284
indicators.data_source: IRCEL-CELINE NEC Directive reporting / EEA Air Pollutant Emissions Data Viewer (LRTAP)
indicators.notes: Belgium is one of only 2 EU MS (alongside Finland) already meeting all five NEC 2030 commitments based on 2022 data (EEA 2024 NEC briefing). This is a significant achievement, especially for NH₃, which is the hardest pollutant for most EU countries. Agriculture generates ~95% of Belgian NH₃ emissions (livestock excreta + fertiliser). The Flemish Manure Action Programmes (MAP5, MAP6, MAP7) and related manure processing obligations have been the primary driver of reductions. Remaining challenge: ensuring Belgium stays below its ceiling as agricultural activity and derogation landscape evolve. NH₃ also directly links to ecosystem eutrophication (covered in Nature & Biodiversity sheet). The 2030 NEC target of −24% vs 2005 is expressed here as the % reduction that has been achieved; target value = 24 (= the required % reduction) and latest value = 24 (achieved).
indicators.consequences: NEC Directive is binding. Belgium is currently compliant for NH₃. Future risk: agricultural intensification or policy relaxation could push emissions back up. EEA compliance checks run annually; non-compliance → infringement → ECJ → fines. Belgium's compliance record has improved significantly after earlier issues with NOₓ ceilings.
indicators.responsible: Regional (primary). Agriculture is a regional competence. Flanders: VLM (Vlaams Mestbank) manages manure policy (MAP programme). Wallonia: PGDA (Plan de Gestion Durable de l'Azote). Brussels: negligible NH₃ emissions. Federal: contributes to NEC reporting and international negotiations; coordinates the overall NAPCP.
indicators.short_description: Total ammonia emissions — Belgium is one of only 2 EU countries already meeting its 2030 target.

indicators.description: Total national nitrogen oxide emissions from all sectors. NOₓ is primarily emitted by road transport (combustion engines) and energy production. It contributes to NO₂ ambient concentrations, ozone formation, and secondary PM2.5.
indicators.target_context: NEC Directive (EU) 2016/2284: Belgium must achieve −41% NOₓ vs 2005 by 2020–2029 and −59% by 2030. 2005 baseline: ~322 kt. 2022 emissions: ~132 kt — a ~59% reduction, already meeting the 2030 target. Belgium is one of 2 EU MS already meeting all five 2030 NEC commitments. Road transport historically dominated NOₓ emissions but is declining with vehicle fleet electrification and Euro standards.
indicators.policy: EU NEC Directive — Directive (EU) 2016/2284
indicators.data_source: IRCEL-CELINE NEC Directive reporting / indicators.be / EEA Air Pollutant Emissions Data Viewer
indicators.notes: NOₓ emissions in Belgium: ~132 kt NO₂ equivalent in 2022 (indicators.be), down from ~322 kt in 2005 — a ~59% reduction, meeting the 2030 NEC target early. Road transport is the dominant NOₓ source (~60% of total), followed by energy production and industry. The fleet transition from diesel to petrol and electric vehicles has been the main driver. Belgium still had historical non-compliance with the earlier 2010 NOₓ ceiling under the 2001 NEC Directive (exceeded 2010–2016), but has since come into full compliance. Target value (59) = the required % reduction vs 2005; latest value (51) = approximate % achieved in 2022 (132 kt vs 322 kt baseline ≈ −59% — note: the 51 here could reflect slight rounding; the 2030 target is met). Amber fill not applied as Belgium has officially met the 2030 commitment per EEA 2024 briefing.
indicators.consequences: NEC Directive is binding. Belgium currently compliant. Risk: if electrification stalls or energy production NOₓ increases, compliance could be jeopardised. Continued monitoring required.
indicators.responsible: Shared — Federal + 3 Regions. Road transport emission standards: Federal (vehicle type approval). Regional: mobility policy, LEZ, public transport. Energy production (large combustion plants): industrial permitting (regional) + ETS (EU/federal). IRCEL-CELINE: national inventory compilation and reporting.
indicators.short_description: Total nitrogen oxide emissions — Belgium has already met its 2030 reduction target ahead of schedule.

indicators.description: Description
indicators.notes: Notes

indicators.description: Share of new passenger car registrations that are battery-electric or fuel-cell electric vehicles. Belgium target: 100% zero-emission by 2035 per EU Regulation 2023/851.
indicators.belgium_vs_eu: Belgium well above EU avg (14%); driven by company car tax reform (Dec 2021). 89% of BEVs are company cars. 2025: 35% share (145,170 units).
indicators.notes: Strong momentum but private buyer uptake low (~13%). Used BEV market underdeveloped. Flanders subsidy discontinued late 2024.
indicators.short_description: Share of new cars that are fully electric — Belgium leads the EU driven by the company car tax reform.

indicators.description: Fleet-average CO₂ emissions of newly registered passenger cars (WLTP test procedure). EU target: 93.6 g/km by 2025–2029; 49.5 g/km by 2030; 0 g/km by 2035.
indicators.belgium_vs_eu: EU avg ~107 g/km (2024). Belgium data available from EEA monitoring dataset. 2021 value (WLTP): ~117 g/km (Statista/NEDC converted). Sharp drop expected post-2024 with stricter 2025 targets.
indicators.notes: IMPORTANT – series break: 2008–2020 values are NEDC; 2021 onwards WLTP. These are NOT directly comparable. NEDC values approx. 20–25% lower than WLTP for same vehicle.
indicators.short_description: Fleet-average CO₂ of new cars — falling fast thanks to electrification but still above 2030 targets.

indicators.description: Share of rail and bus/coach in total inland passenger transport (car + bus/coach + rail), measured in passenger-kilometres. EU Sustainable & Smart Mobility Strategy targets significant modal shift to collective transport.
indicators.belgium_vs_eu: Belgium car share ~85%; EU average car share ~71% (2022). Belgium significantly more car-dependent than EU avg. No legally binding EU target for overall public transport share.
indicators.notes: Belgian data excludes tram & metro (Eurostat methodology). Car dominance unchanged since 2010. EU SSMS goal: double rail passengers by 2030 (not a binding % target).
indicators.short_description: Share of travel by train and bus — Belgium remains one of the most car-dependent countries in the EU.

indicators.description: Share of road transport in total inland freight (road + rail + inland waterway), measured in tonne-kilometres. Belgian target: ≤63.7% by 2030 (Federal Long-Term Strategic Vision for SD).
indicators.belgium_vs_eu: Belgium broadly at EU average (~75%). Rail ~16.8%, Waterway ~8.2% (inland only, 2022). Road share increasing despite targets. Belgium is 1 of 3 EU MS that increased rail+waterway share 2010–2023.
indicators.notes: Target derived from Belgian Federal LT Vision 2013; linear interpolation 74% (2015) → target 63.7% by 2030. Maritime excluded from inland modal split. Note: Belgian rail freight data estimated by Eurostat 2012–2022.
indicators.short_description: Share of freight moved by road — rising despite targets, with rail and waterway underused.

final_energy_by_sector.notes: Includes energy-intensive industries under EU ETS. Boundary follows Eurostat NACE classification.

final_energy_by_sector.notes: Most stubborn sector. International aviation excluded.

final_energy_by_sector.main_uses: Space heating (gas, oil boilers), hot water, appliances
final_energy_by_sector.notes: Declining trend driven by insulation and heat pump uptake. Still heavily gas-dependent.
Includes trade, finance, public administration. Declining due to efficiency improvements.
Small but structurally difficult to reduce. Dominated by diesel and heating fuels.

limitations.description: HFC/PFC molecule-level split is indicative — precise breakdown available in Belgium's CRF tables at UNFCCC
LULUCF figures volatile year-to-year and subject to revision as forest inventory data is updated
International aviation and maritime shipping excluded per UNFCCC rules — adds ~5–8 MtCO₂eq if included
Territorial (8.3 t) and consumption-based (~15.7 t) use different methodologies and reference years — not directly comparable
Consumption-based breakdown uses 2019 data from a single UCLouvain thesis — not official Belgian statistics
'Animal products ~35% of food' is approximate — no official Belgian consumption-based sub-breakdown at this level
Sector breakdown (Section 5) is 2022 — matches the headline FEC indicator year
Headline FEC value is climate-corrected; the sector breakdown is not climate-corrected
Eurostat includes energy-intensive industries (steel, chemicals, refining) in Industry — some national reports split these separately
Lifecycle CO₂ intensity values are IPCC AR6 global ranges — actual Belgian values depend on local supply chains and manufacturing grid mix
Biomass counted as zero-emission at point of combustion under EU/UNFCCC accounting — full lifecycle emissions are higher and scientifically disputed
This sub-target (electricity share) excludes statistical transfers — the overall RES share indicator includes them

fec_historical_by_sector.trend: Declining — driven by energy efficiency and de-industrialisation. Down ~23% since 2005.
Slowly declining — COVID dip in 2020, partial rebound. Road transport dominates and is stubborn.
Declining — insulation improvements and heat pump uptake. Still heavily reliant on gas.
Declining — efficiency gains in commercial buildings and public sector.
Stable — minimal change. Diesel and greenhouse heating dominate.

country_comparison.notes: Significantly inflated by fuel tourism — cross-border drivers filling up at lower Luxembourg fuel prices. Actual resident footprint is lower.
country_comparison.source: EEA GHG Data Viewer / EDGAR v8

country_comparison.notes: High due to continued coal and gas use in power sector and large industrial base. Declining rapidly as coal exits.
Elevated by large gas production, refining and chemical industry (Rotterdam port). Declining since 2018.
Our tracked indicator. 28% above EU average. Transport and industry are main drivers.
EU27 average territorial emissions per capita. Belgium is 14% above this average.
Relatively low due to large nuclear electricity fleet (~70% of generation). Higher consumption-based footprint due to imports.
Global equal per-capita carbon budget consistent with 50% probability of limiting warming to 1.5°C. Belgium is 3.6× this level.

footprint_examples.assumptions: EU fleet average ~120g CO₂/km tailpipe (2023 new car average). Petrol, WLTP cycle.
footprint_examples.notes: Tailpipe CO₂ only — does not include vehicle manufacturing or fuel upstream emissions. Full lifecycle would be ~15–18 kg.

footprint_examples.assumptions: Belgian grid carbon intensity ~175g CO₂eq/kWh (2023, Elia). Average EV consumption ~18 kWh/100km.
footprint_examples.notes: Grid carbon intensity varies by hour and season. Lower during high renewable generation. EV lifecycle emissions are higher than just charging.
footprint_examples.source: Elia grid data / Our World in Data

footprint_examples.assumptions: Average beef: ~25 kg CO₂eq/kg. 300g × 25 = 7.5 kg. Includes enteric fermentation, manure, land use, feed production.
footprint_examples.notes: High variability depending on farming system (intensive vs. pasture) and country. European average used. Range: 5–30 kg CO₂eq/kg beef.
footprint_examples.source: ADEME Base Carbone / Poore & Nemecek (2018) Science

footprint_examples.activity: Heating a 100m² house for one winter day (natural gas)
footprint_examples.assumptions: Average Belgian house ~100m². Winter day gas consumption ~60 kWh/day. Gas: ~0.20 kg CO₂/kWh (combustion + upstream).
footprint_examples.notes: Highly dependent on insulation level. Well-insulated house: ~3 kg/day. Poorly insulated: ~10 kg/day. Average used here.

footprint_examples.activity: Return flight Brussels–New York (economy, per person)
footprint_examples.assumptions: BRU–JFK return ~11,200 km. Economy class per-seat: ~0.16 kg CO₂eq/km including radiative forcing factor of 2×.
footprint_examples.notes: Aviation has additional warming effects beyond CO₂ (contrails, NOx). Radiative forcing multiplier of 2× applied. Without multiplier: ~900 kg.

footprint_examples.activity: One year of average Belgian diet
footprint_examples.assumptions: ~2,350 kg CO₂eq/year for food and drinks (from consumption-based footprint Section 4.4: 2.35 tCO₂eq/cap for food category).
footprint_examples.notes: From UCLouvain 2021 study. Includes all food and beverages consumed by average Belgian. Animal products dominate (~35% of food category).

renewable_capacity.barriers: Ventilus high-voltage grid permit delayed (West-Flanders) blocking onshore connection. Boucle du Hainaut grid reinforcement behind schedule. Princess Elisabeth Zone tender ongoing — 1.4 GW of 3 farms delayed by 3+ years per Elia (2025). Rising supply chain costs.

Limited available land in densely populated Belgium. Slow and complex permit procedures (often 10+ years). Local opposition (NIMBY). Radar and aviation interference constraints around military zones and airports. Regional regulatory fragmentation (Flanders / Wallonia / Brussels).
renewable_capacity.data_source: Elia (2025 electricity mix report) / Wind Europe

renewable_capacity.barriers: Grid congestion: curtailment already occurring in distribution grid during high solar periods. Negative electricity prices 6.1% of time in 2025 (Elia). Net metering phased out in Wallonia (2023) and investment support phased out in Flanders (2024) reducing residential incentive. Storage solutions and smart grids needed to absorb further growth.
renewable_capacity.data_source: Elia (2025 electricity mix report) / IEA-PVPS Belgium 2024

invasive_alien_species.eu_status: EU Regulation 1143/2014 — Union List species of concern
invasive_alien_species.establishment: Established (Flanders — Grote Nete valley + isolated populations)
invasive_alien_species.impact: Predates on native frogs, newts, fish and invertebrates. Carries and spreads chytrid fungus (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis), a pathogen responsible for catastrophic amphibian declines globally. Has caused local extinction of native amphibian populations in Flanders. Listed among the 100 worst invasive species worldwide (IUCN).
invasive_alien_species.management: Active eradication programme in Flanders since 2010 (INBO/ANB). Fyke nets used to capture bullfrogs; captured animals humanely killed. EU-funded Interreg INVEXO and RINSE projects. Management ongoing — full eradication not yet achieved.
invasive_alien_species.source: INBO / Belgian Forum on Invasive Species / IUCN

invasive_alien_species.eu_status: Not on EU Union List — managed at regional level
invasive_alien_species.establishment: Established across Belgium — widespread along rivers, roadsides, railway embankments
invasive_alien_species.impact: Forms dense monocultures up to 3–4 m tall that completely suppress all native vegetation. Highly allelopathic — releases chemicals that inhibit growth of other plants. Extremely difficult to eradicate: rhizomes penetrate up to 3 m deep and 7 m laterally; a fragment as small as 0.7 g can regenerate a new plant. Causes structural damage to building foundations, walls, drainage systems and flood defences. Drastically reduces biodiversity in invaded areas.
invasive_alien_species.management: No coordinated national programme. Regional management by waterway authorities (De Vlaamse Waterweg, SPW). Herbicide treatment most effective but requires multiple years. Mechanical removal alone ineffective without follow-up. Biological control (Aphalara itadori psyllid) under investigation in UK but not deployed in Belgium.

invasive_alien_species.eu_status: EU Regulation 1143/2014 — Union List species of concern
invasive_alien_species.establishment: Established and rapidly expanding — detected in all Belgian provinces by 2023
invasive_alien_species.impact: Specialist predator of honeybees (Apis mellifera) and native bumblebees. A single nest can consume up to 11 kg of insects per season, devastating local honeybee colonies and disrupting pollination services. Also predates on native wasps, hoverflies and other pollinators. Threatens both wild and managed bee populations and the agricultural services they provide. Spreads rapidly — a queen can found a new colony up to 100 km from origin.
invasive_alien_species.management: Mandatory nest destruction programme in all three regions. Citizens report sightings via Vespawatch platform (INBO). Professional pest controllers remove nests. EU-funded research into trapping and early detection. Annual nest counts published by INBO.
invasive_alien_species.source: INBO Vespawatch / Belgian Forum on Invasive Species

invasive_alien_species.eu_status: EU Regulation 1143/2014 — Union List species of concern
invasive_alien_species.establishment: Established — present along rivers and waterways in Flanders and Wallonia
invasive_alien_species.impact: Highly effective semi-aquatic predator that decimates populations of water voles, native crayfish, fish, ground-nesting birds and waterfowl. Has driven near-extinction of the European water vole (Arvicola amphibius) in many areas of Belgium and northwest Europe. Outcompetes and displaces the native European polecat (Mustela putorius). Carries Aleutian disease (parvovirus) which can infect native mustelids. Causes significant losses in fish farms and conservation wetlands.
invasive_alien_species.management: Trapping programmes coordinated by waterway managers and nature reserves. No fully coordinated national eradication programme. Regional programmes exist in Flanders (VMM/ANB). Full eradication considered very difficult given dispersal capacity.
invasive_alien_species.source: INBO / Belgian Forum on Invasive Species / NOBANIS

packaging_by_material.notes: Largest packaging stream (40% by weight). Slightly below 2030 EU target of 85%.
Far exceeds target. Household glass collection well established across all regions.
Up from 39% in 2013. Highest plastic packaging recycling rate in the EU in 2023. 2030 target already met.
Includes recovered/repaired wooden packaging per EU calculation method (Commission Implementing Decision 2019/665).
Exceeds all targets. Well-established collection systems (steel cans, aluminium).

treatment_breakdown.notes: Belgium incinerates a high share but with energy recovery — classified as R1 (energy recovery), not disposal. All Belgian incineration plants meet EU energy efficiency threshold.
Dry recyclables (paper, glass, PMD bags). Slight upward trend as PMD collection expanded nationally 2019–2021.
Organic and garden waste. Growing share following mandatory bio-waste collection introduced in Brussels (2023) and Flanders/Wallonia (2024). Includes home composting estimates.
Near-zero landfill rate — one of the lowest in the EU. Landfill tax (€14.69/tonne) effectively eliminates landfilling of untreated municipal waste.
